articles.title: Vercel Acquires NuxtLabs
articles.description: Vercel has acquired the company that caretakes the Nuxt project and employs some of its core team – a move Vue creator Evan You is quite optimistic about. Vercel now manages, or at least supports, several key projects like Next.js, Turborepo, Svelte, and shadcn/ui. Nuxt itself remains open source and has a promising future. Vercel’s Guillermo Rauch shares a little more about the move here.
articles.author: NuxtLabs / Vercel

articles.title: A Detailed Summary of the Latest TC39 Plenary
articles.description: A thorough roundup of May’s major ECMAScript committee meeting with far more detail about each proposal’s development and the decisions made than we usually get to hear about. Topics include Array.fromAsync, explicit resource management, the Temporal API, and some brainstorming around AsyncContext.
articles.author: Igalia Compilers Team

articles.title: What’s the Difference Between Ordinary Functions and Arrow Functions?
articles.description: This sounds like basic stuff, but James always does a good job of digging in and explaining things in a way that gives you a more nuanced way to think about a concept, even if it’s just “Which function declaration syntax should I use?”
articles.author: James Sinclair

articles.title: JavaScript Scope Hoisting is Broken
articles.description: The creator of Parcel argues that scope hoisting (when bundlers inline modules into a shared scope) conflicts with modern JS patterns like code splitting and dynamic imports, causing subtle bugs and offering little benefit, so he’s considering removing it in Parcel v3.
articles.author: Devon Govett

articles.title: Codepoint–Safe Truncation: Fixing Emoji Slicing
articles.description: An app’s CSV importer kept breaking on emoji-filled rows, triggering errors. James demonstrates how swapping slice for a code-point–aware spread fixes it.
articles.author: James Mulholland

articles.title: Driver.js: Tours, Highlights, Contextual Help, and More
articles.description: A vanilla JS library for making on-page tours and contextual help systems. It’s been around for several years, but is still maintained, and there are lots of examples to check out – it’s really smooth.
articles.author: Kamran Ahmed

articles.title: jsonrepair: Repair Invalid JSON Documents
articles.description: This has lots of possible use cases, including dealing with weird JSON coming back from LLMs or non-compliant JSON spat out by poorly built software. You can use it from Node, as a CLI tool, or try a basic version online.
articles.author: Jos de Jong

articles.title: line-numbers: A Web Component to Add Line Numbers Next to Various HTML Elements
articles.description: Useful for custom apps that show source code or other snippets that require line numbering. See examples here, which demonstrate the flexibility customization options for the line numbering.
articles.author: Zach Leatherman

articles.title: cRonstrue 3.0: Convert Cron Expressions into Natural Language
articles.description: Not just English either - it supports about thirty locales. There’s also an online demo.
articles.author: Brady Holt

articles.description: TypeScript 5.9 Beta has been released with support for deferred module evaluation by using import defer.

The Nginx HTTP server has a module (njs) for extending its functionality using JavaScript, but it only supported ES5. Now, njs uses QuickJS for a more modern, powerful experience with full ES2023 support.
Got some time to kill? There's one week left to submit an entry to the JS1024 annual JavaScript golfing competition. Create a cool JS or GLSL program in under 1024 bytes.
Oliver Stenbom reflects on the prevalence of Rust in modern JavaScript ecosystem tooling.
Node.js v24.4.0 (Current) – Note that new versions of all maintained Node.js releases are due next week due to some security vulnerabilities.
Oxlint 1.6 – Rust-powered linter for JS and TS.
VS Code v1.102, Ember 6.5, Angular 20.1

articles.title: "I’m More Proud of These 128 Kilobytes Than Anything I’ve Built Since"
articles.description: Here’s a solid recounting of a project that highlights just how strict constraints (such as bandwidth, processing power, etc) can often result in innovation. It’s also a sound reminder to us all to consider the widest range of users and to design things accordingly.
articles.author: Mike Hall

articles.title: Apple’s Browser Engine Ban Persists, Even Under the DMA
articles.description: Argues that Apple is not effectively complying with the EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA) regarding browser engine choice on iOS: “Apple knows exactly what the barriers are, and has chosen not to remove them”.
articles.author: Open Web Advocacy

articles.title: WebAssembly: Yes, But for What?
articles.description: Writing for ACM Queue, a contributor to multiple JavaScript and WASM implementations shares a good roundup of where WebAssembly is being used, both in the browser and server-side, and how it’s ultimately finding its way into seemingly everything.
articles.author: Andy Wingo / ACM

articles.title: Setting Line Length in CSS (and Fitting Text to a Container)
articles.description: Thanks to tools like clamp(), calc(), and others it’s now much easier to set line length than it was just a few years ago. Daniel looks over the options and how to use them, along with a look ahead at what the future may have in store.
articles.author: Daniel Schwarz

articles.title: ▶  Multicol and Fragmentation
articles.description: A near hour-long talk from this year's CSS Day in which Rachel Andrew, an author on the spec, shares an update on the CSS multiple-column layout specification — diving into columns, boxes, fragmentation, browser support, and more.
articles.author: Rachel Andrew

articles.title: Scroll-Driven Sticky Heading
articles.description: Amit notes that CSS scroll-driven animations are “more than just a cool trick; they’re a game-changer that opens the door to a whole new world of web design”. This post shares one such example of the dynamic, engaging things they make possible (although browser support isn’t universal just yet).
articles.author: Amit Sheen

articles.title: Stacked Transforms
articles.description: Chris takes a look over the animation-composition CSS property, detailing how it affects transforms during animations. An interesting breakdown, and something Chris notes "will come in useful someday [..] You won’t need it until you need it".
articles.author: Chris Coyier

articles.title: Please, Can We Have ARIA Notify
articles.description: “why I’m hoping and praying with every fiber of my being that we will get the ARIA Notify API across all browsers at some point in the future”.
articles.author: Nic Chan

articles.title: SveltePlot: A Visualization Framework for Svelte
articles.description: The API is inspired by a similar plotting library by Observable and is built on top of D3. There are plenty of good examples in the docs, including flow plots, scatterplots, maps, streamgraphs and more.
articles.author: Gregor Aisch

articles.title: Eleventy LibDoc: An Eleventy Starter Project to Craft Slick Documentation
articles.description: The docs site for the project is an example of the attractive and intuitive layout it produces. It’s accessible, includes search, has syntax highlighting, among lots of other features.
articles.author: IT Automotive Design System

articles.title: SplitThing: Upload an Image and Split It Into a Custom Grid
articles.description: A simple tool with a specific use case. You can use sliders to define the number of columns and rows for the grid, then download the sliced up images as a ZIP file.
articles.author: Florian Reintgen

articles.title: Chili3D: A Web-Based 3D CAD App for Online Model Design and Editing
articles.description: Impressively claims to achieve “near-native performance” which it does by compiling OpenCascade to WebAssembly and integrating with Three.js. You can judge for yourself by trying it out.
articles.author: xiange chen

articles.title: designtokens.fyi: A Glossary of Terms Related to Design Tokens
articles.description: A well-presented little info site currently with 33 terms explained including common things like “custom property”, used in CSS, and not-so-common stuff like “smedium”, a problem with maintaining scales.
articles.author: Design Systems House
